description: Figure 2 presents the tensile mechanical response of Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low-density steel after solution treatment at 700°C, 800°C, 900°C, and 1000°C (corresponding to specimens H70, H80, H90, and H100, respectively). Panel (a) displays true stress–strain curves with true stress in MPa versus true strain: no yield point phenomenon is visually apparent for any condition. As the solution temperature increases, the initial yield stress decreases progressively, while the 900°C curve shows a pronounced stress inflection at a true strain of ~0.03 and attains the highest true stress (~1120 MPa) and largest uniform elongation before fracture. Panel (b) plots the corresponding work-hardening rate (dσ/dε) against true strain, revealing distinct three-stage work-hardening behavior labeled S1, S2, and S3 across all temperatures. Stage S1 exhibits a sharp drop in work-hardening rate immediately after yielding. In stage S2, the 900°C curve uniquely shows a rising or plateau-like work-hardening rate between true strains of ~0.03 and 0.15, whereas the 700°C, 800°C, and 1000°C curves display only a gradual decline; this S2 behavior is attributed to a strain-induced martensitic transformation (TRIP effect). Stage S3 is characterized by a renewed rapid decrease in work-hardening rate, linked to deformation localization. The caption and supporting text infer that the absence of mechanical twinning and the pronounced S2 stage in the 900°C specimen indicate the TRIP effect as the dominant mechanism responsible for the superior strength–ductility combination.
